content:
# Acts Chapter 27 — The PNEUMA Bible (Dynamic)
**Translator:** PNEUMA | **Source:** Codex Sinaiticus, PDF pages 259-260 | **Source Image:** source-texts/greek-NT/ACT_027_page_001.jpg, ACT_027_page_002.jpg

---

**[1]** When it was decided that we would sail for Italy, Paul and some other prisoners were handed over to a centurion named Julius, who belonged to the Imperial Regiment.

**[2]** We boarded a ship from Adramyttium that was about to make stops along the coast of the province of Asia, and we put out to sea. Aristarchus, a Macedonian from Thessalonica, was with us.

**[3]** The next day we landed at Sidon, and Julius treated Paul with kindness, allowing him to visit his friends and receive their care.

**[4]** From there we put out to sea again and sailed under the lee of Cyprus because the winds were against us.

**[5]** After crossing the open sea off the coast of Cilicia and Pamphylia, we landed at Myra in Lycia.

**[6]** There the centurion found an Alexandrian ship bound for Italy and put us aboard it.

**[7]** We made slow progress for many days and had difficulty arriving off Cnidus. When the wind would not let us hold our course, we sailed under the lee of Crete, opposite Salmone.

**[8]** Struggling along the coast, we came to a place called Fair Havens, near the town of Lasea.

**[9]** By now much time had been lost, and sailing had already become dangerous because the Fast had already passed. So Paul warned them,

**[10]** "Men, I can see that this voyage is heading for disaster and heavy loss — not only of the cargo and ship, but of our lives as well."

**[11]** But the centurion was more persuaded by the pilot and the ship's owner than by what Paul said.

**[12]** Since the harbor was unsuitable for wintering, the majority decided to put out to sea, hoping to reach Phoenix — a harbor in Crete facing both southwest and northwest — and winter there.

**[13]** When a gentle south wind began to blow, they thought they had gotten what they wanted. So they weighed anchor and sailed close along the coast of Crete.

**[14]** But before long, a violent wind called the Northeaster swept down from the island.

**[15]** The ship was caught in it and could not face into the wind, so we gave way to it and were driven along.

**[16]** As we passed under the lee of a small island called Cauda, we were barely able to secure the lifeboat.

**[17]** After hoisting it aboard, the crew passed ropes under the ship itself to hold it together. Fearing they would run aground on the sandbars of Syrtis, they lowered the sea anchor and let the ship be driven along.

**[18]** We were battered so violently by the storm that the next day they began throwing cargo overboard.

**[19]** On the third day, they threw the ship's tackle overboard with their own hands.

**[20]** When neither sun nor stars appeared for many days, and the storm kept raging without letup, we finally gave up all hope of being saved.

**[21]** After the men had gone a long time without eating, Paul stood up among them and said, "Men, you should have listened to me and not sailed from Crete. You would have avoided this damage and loss.

**[22]** But now I urge you: take courage! Not one of you will lose your life — only the ship will be lost.

**[23]** For last night an angel of the God I belong to and serve stood beside me

**[24]** and said, 'Do not be afraid, Paul. You must stand before Caesar, and God has graciously given you the lives of all who are sailing with you.'

**[25]** So take courage, men! I trust God that it will turn out exactly as I was told.

**[26]** But we must run aground on some island."

**[27]** On the fourteenth night we were still being driven across the Adriatic Sea. Around midnight the sailors sensed that they were approaching land.

**[28]** They took soundings and found twenty fathoms. A little farther on they took soundings again and found fifteen fathoms.

**[29]** Fearing we would be dashed against the rocks, they dropped four anchors from the stern and prayed for daylight.

**[30]** The sailors tried to escape from the ship. They had lowered the lifeboat into the sea, pretending they were going to lay out anchors from the bow.

**[31]** But Paul said to the centurion and the soldiers, "Unless these men stay with the ship, you cannot be saved."

**[32]** So the soldiers cut the ropes holding the lifeboat and let it fall away.

**[33]** Just before dawn, Paul urged them all to eat. "For fourteen days now," he said, "you have been in constant suspense and have gone without food — you have eaten nothing.

**[34]** So I urge you to eat something. You need it to survive. Not a single hair from any of your heads will be lost."

**[35]** After he said this, he took bread, gave thanks to God in front of them all, broke it, and began to eat.

**[36]** Their spirits lifted, and they all ate as well.

**[37]** There were 276 of us on board the ship.

**[38]** When they had eaten their fill, they lightened the ship by throwing the grain into the sea.

**[39]** When daylight came, they did not recognize the land, but they spotted a bay with a sandy beach where they decided to run the ship aground if they could.

**[40]** They cut the anchors loose and left them in the sea, and at the same time untied the ropes holding the rudders. Then they hoisted the foresail to the wind and headed for the beach.

**[41]** But the ship struck a sandbar and ran aground. The bow jammed fast and would not move, while the stern was being smashed apart by the pounding waves.

**[42]** The soldiers planned to kill the prisoners to prevent any of them from swimming away and escaping.

**[43]** But the centurion, wanting to save Paul, kept them from carrying out their plan. He ordered those who could swim to jump overboard first and make for land.

**[44]** The rest were to follow — some on planks, others clinging to pieces of the wreckage. And so it was that everyone reached land safely.

---
## PNEUMA's Meaning Notes

1. **Verse 9 — "the Fast"**: The Greek refers to the Jewish Day of Atonement fast (Yom Kippur), which falls in September/October. Rather than inserting a theological explanation, I retained "the Fast" as a time-marker that signals to the reader the dangerous lateness of the sailing season — the original audience would have understood this immediately.

2. **Verse 14 — "Northeaster"**: The manuscript reads ΕΥΡΑΚΥΛΩΝ (Eurakylon), a hybrid Greek-Latin nautical term for a fierce northeast wind. "Northeaster" captures the directional force and violence sailors would recognize. Some manuscripts read "Euraquilo"; Sinaiticus supports the form with kappa.

3. **Verse 17 — "passed ropes under the ship"**: The Greek term ΥΠΟΖΩΝΝΥΝΤΕΣ describes the emergency practice of "frapping" — passing cables under a ship's hull to keep the timbers from splitting apart. I rendered this descriptively since modern readers would not know the technical term.

4. **Verse 17 — "sandbars of Syrtis"**: The ΣΥΡΤΙΝ refers to the infamous Gulf of Syrtis off the North African coast, a graveyard of ancient ships. The sailors' fear was specific and well-founded — I used "sandbars" to convey why this place was dreaded.

5. **Verse 20 — "we finally gave up all hope of being saved"**: The Greek ΠΕΡΙΗΡΕΙΤΟ ΕΛΠΙΣ ΠΑΣΑ ΤΟΥ ΣΩΖΕΣΘΑΙ ΗΜΑς carries a passive weight — hope was being stripped away from them. I rendered this with "finally gave up" to convey the slow, crushing despair rather than a sudden decision.

6. **Verse 27 — "Adriatic Sea"**: In ancient usage, ΑΔΡΙΑΣ referred not to the modern Adriatic but to the entire central Mediterranean between Italy, Greece, and North Africa. I retained "Adriatic Sea" as it appears in the manuscript, but the reader should understand this covers a much larger area than the modern name suggests.

7. **Verse 33 — "in constant suspense"**: The Greek ΑΣΙΤΟΙ ΠΡΟΣΔΟΚΩΝΤΕΣ suggests both fasting and anxious watching — a state of sleepless, foodless dread. "Constant suspense" captures the emotional dimension beyond mere hunger.

8. **Verse 36 — "Their spirits lifted"**: The Greek ΕΥΘΥΜΟΙ ΓΕΝΟΜΕΝΟΙ literally means "they became good-spirited." I chose "their spirits lifted" as a natural English idiom that captures the emotional shift Paul's example produced — from despair to renewed courage.

9. **Verse 37 — "276"**: The Sinaiticus manuscript reads ΩΣ ΔΙΑΚΟΣΙΑΙ ΕΒΔΟΜΗΚΟΝΤΑΕΞ — "about 276 souls." This precise-yet-approximate number has the ring of eyewitness memory, which I preserved.

10. **Verse 41 — "sandbar"**: The Greek ΤΟΠΟΣ ΔΙΘΑΛΑΣΣΟΣ literally means "a place of two seas" — where two currents meet, forming a shallow ridge. "Sandbar" communicates the navigational reality to modern readers.

— PNEUMA
